meta.title: AI Architecture Principles — Taxonomy and Schema
meta.description: Five-pillar taxonomy, focus areas, and per-principle schema specification for the principles catalogue (principles.json). Mirrors the AWS Well-Architected Generative AI Lens structure (with sustainability omitted) so clients fluent in AWS guidance can apply this catalogue without translation. The catalogue is anchored to AWS today but the schema is vendor-neutral and can equally hold Azure or GCP mappings via framework_mappings.
notes: Pillars are top-level concerns. Focus areas are groupings of related questions and best practices within each pillar. Both terms align with AWS terminology. Pillars are coded P1–P5. Focus areas inherit the pillar code as a prefix (P11 is the first focus area of P1; P23 is the third focus area of P2; and so on).

Focus area names approximate the AWS GenAI Lens question areas. GENOPS focus areas (P11–P15) are aligned to the Lens as of May 2026. GENSEC / GENREL / GENPERF / GENCOST focus area names require a verification pass against current Lens documentation before being treated as locked — each focus area carries an aws_alignment_status flag making this explicit.
Historical 'Why the enforcement field was retired' section from taxonomy.md has been dropped; that history is captured by the 2026-05-24 schema-v1.2 entry in decisions.md.

Schema v1.6 (2026-05-26) re-introduced two root-level filtering fields on every principle: `applicability` (which AI patterns the principle applies to — llm/rag/agentic/ml) and `maturity_level` (when an adopting organisation should care — foundational/scaling/mature). These existed in the legacy v1.0 schema, were dropped during the v1.5 rebuild while framework_mappings was being finalised, and brought back after the GENOPS01-BP01 walkthrough surfaced that adopters need to distinguish must-have-day-1 principles from must-have-at-scale ones. See conventions.applicability_and_maturity_level.

Schema v1.7 (2026-05-26) reshaped `applicability` from a flat array of patterns (e.g. `['agentic']`) to a pattern-criticality map (e.g. `{ 'agentic': 'mandatory' }`). The map keys are still drawn from `enums.applicability` (llm/rag/agentic/ml); the map values come from a new `enums.pattern_criticality` (mandatory/nice_to_have). This lets adopters filter not just by 'does this principle apply to my pattern' but by 'is it mandatory or nice-to-have for my pattern' — a question the array shape could not answer. See conventions.choosing_pattern_criticality.

Schema v1.8 (2026-05-26) added an optional `explain_prompt` field on every principle, holding a compiled OpenAI-style system + user_template pair used by the runtime UI's Problem-tab 'Explain' affordance to generate illustrative use-case + issue-detail examples for a chosen industry and implementation type. The compiled prompt is principle-specific: it bakes in the principle's statement, problem, examples, applicability map, and — most importantly — the shape of the failure mode the principle uniquely prevents (silent regression, setup absence, cumulative drift, one-shot breach, etc.). This shifts the prompt-engineering work off the runtime path: a generic universal prompt could not honour every failure shape without distorting examples, so each principle carries its own. See conventions.per_principle_explain_prompt_compilation and usage.when_compiling_an_explain_prompt.

Schema document format_version 1.4 (2026-05-27) extended `conventions` to declare `per_field_authoring_rubrics` — every LLM-authored field carries a 0–3 dimensioned quality rubric, implemented in `agentflow/rubrics/`. Rubrics are catalogue-authoring discipline (what the agentflow pipeline does when generating principle content), not per-principle content rules, so they do not appear as principles in principles.json. No change to `principle_schema` itself or to `applies_to_principles_schema_version` (stays at 1.8) — this is a convention addition, not a per-principle schema change. See conventions.per_field_authoring_rubrics and the 2026-05-27 entry in decisions.md.

Schema document format_version 1.5 (2026-05-27) changed the `principle_id` format from `PRIN_NNN` to a BP-anchored, sequential convention: `<bp_code>-NN` where `bp_code` compresses the AWS pillar / question / BP triple to 5 characters (e.g. `GO1B1` = GenOps / Q1 / BP1) and `NN` is a sequential counter — the Nth principle promoted from that BP, in promotion order. IDs within a BP are consecutive (01, 02, 03, …) with no gaps. The AWS implementation step a principle concretises is recorded in `framework_mappings.aws.references[0].implementation_step.number`, not in the principle_id suffix. The four existing principles (formerly PRIN_001 / PRIN_002 / PRIN_003 / PRIN_004) were renamed to GO1B1-01 / GO1B1-02 / GO1B1-03 / GO1B1-04 with PATCH bumps. The rename surfaces BP membership in the ID itself — `framework_mappings.aws.references[0].best_practice` was the only place the BP grouping was visible, and required reading every principle to recover the BP-shaped view. Historical references in append-only journals (decisions.md prose, change_history summaries) were left as-is; live cross-references (lens_mapping.md tables, governance.json related_principle fields, classstrategy.md, agentflow/rubrics calibration examples) were updated. No change to `applies_to_principles_schema_version` (stays at 1.8) — the principle_id field's type and required-ness are unchanged, only its documented format. See conventions.principle_id_format and the 2026-05-27 rename entry in decisions.md.

Schema document format_version 1.6 (2026-05-29) extended `conventions` to declare `step_promotion_rubric` — a separate, upstream-decision rubric authored as `agentflow/sections/step_promotion/{generate,rubric,revise}.json`. Unlike `per_field_authoring_rubrics` which scores quality WITHIN an already-promoted principle's authored fields, this rubric scores the upstream binary decision of whether an AWS implementation step becomes a principle at all. Four dimensions: has_enforceable_artefact, architecturally_distinct, in_bp_scope, not_vendor_menu; all-dims-≥-2 threshold. Binary outcome — promote or not_promote, no `merge` category (absorbed content lives inside the sibling principle's statement / solution / gates, which is a sibling-authoring choice rather than a structural outcome of this decomposition pass). No change to `principle_schema` itself or to `applies_to_principles_schema_version` (stays at 1.8) — this is a convention addition, not a per-principle schema change. See conventions.step_promotion_rubric and the 2026-05-29 step_promotion entry in decisions.md.

Schema v1.9 (2026-05-29, document format_version 1.7) added a new required root-level field `serving_paradigm` on every principle — a criticality map (same shape as `applicability` v1.7) keyed by foundation-model serving paradigm. Keys drawn from `enums.serving_paradigm` (api_on_demand / api_provisioned / self_hosted_managed / self_hosted_unmanaged); values drawn from `enums.pattern_criticality` (mandatory / nice_to_have). Surfaced during the GENCOST02-BP02 walkthrough where the candidate GC2B2-01 principle (right-size hosting infrastructure) is intrinsically self-hosting-only — it has no failure mode against pure API workloads (no instances to right-size, no RIs to buy, no shutdowns to schedule). The existing `applicability` map is keyed by AI pattern (llm/rag/agentic/ml) and could not express the serving-paradigm narrowing. The new field is orthogonal to `applicability`: a principle can be mandatory for llm + rag + agentic (applicability) AND only mandatory for self_hosted_managed + self_hosted_unmanaged (serving_paradigm). All six pre-existing principles were retrofitted with serving_paradigm: { api_on_demand: mandatory, api_provisioned: mandatory, self_hosted_managed: mandatory, self_hosted_unmanaged: mandatory } — eval discipline, drift monitoring, refresh, and model-selection ADR all apply regardless of how the model is served. See conventions.choosing_serving_paradigm and the 2026-05-29 serving_paradigm entry in decisions.md.

Schema document format_version 1.8 (2026-06-01) replaces the prior `ownership_spec.enterprise_qualification_rules` 4-rule binary screen with a new tier rubric framework authored at `agentflow/sections/tier/rubric.json` (2026-05-31). Two dimensions: D1 (legal_exposure, binary veto) and D2 (repeatability_cost, 0-3 scored). Three outcomes mapped to the two-value tier enum: `mandatory_centralise` (D1=yes) → tier: enterprise; `recommended_centralise` (D1=no, D2≥2) → tier: enterprise; `local` (D1=no, D2≤1) → tier: project. The four prior binary triggers are subsumed by the rubric (rule 1 ≈ D2 high; rule 2 = D1 veto; rule 3 folds into D2; rule 4 folds into D1) but the new rubric makes reuse cost a first-class scored dimension rather than a binary trigger. The `ownership.tier` field's semantics are also redefined: tier now reflects centralisation of enforcement INFRASTRUCTURE (whether the platform team builds and maintains the runners, lints, validators, CI workflow templates, dashboards that make the principle hold mechanically) — NOT validation. Validation is now an independent axis on `ownership.validator` and `ownership.audit_mode`; an enterprise-tier principle can still have `validator: project_architect` and `audit_mode: self_attestation_with_mechanical_evidence` when the platform team owns the tooling and the PA self-attests using it. Five existing principles were reclassified project → enterprise under the new rubric in principles.json on 2026-05-31: GO1B1-01, GO1B1-02, GO1B1-03, GO1B1-04, GC2B2-01. The `evidence` field's prior `required_when: ownership.tier == 'enterprise'` condition is also updated — under the new tier semantics, an enterprise-tier principle does not automatically require central-review evidence; the trigger is the audit_mode (`central_review_at_gate`), not the tier. `applies_to_principles_schema_version` unchanged at 1.9 — this is an ownership / evidence spec rewrite and a convention replacement, not a per-principle schema-shape change. See the rewritten `ownership_spec` block and the 2026-05-31 tier-rubric entry in decisions.md.

Schema v1.10 (2026-06-05) added a new required root-level field `dependencies` on every principle — an array recording the other catalogue principles this principle DIRECTLY depends on. Each entry is `{ principle_id, kind, reason }` where `kind` is drawn from the new `enums.dependency_kind` (hard / soft): `hard` means the principle cannot be enforced or cannot function without the dependency's substrate in place (e.g. GC3B1-01's token-budget gate cannot bite without GO3B1-01's template registry providing addressable template_ids); `soft` means the principle composes with or is strengthened by the dependency but can be adopted without it. The value is an empty array `[]` for foundational principles that stand alone. Only DIRECT dependencies are listed, not the transitive closure — dependency chains are recoverable by walking the graph. Surfaced 2026-06-05 during the GENCOST03-BP03 (prompt caching) walkthrough: the caching principle reuses GO3B1-01's registry and GC3B1-01's static-prefix token computation, and the catalogue had no structured way to record that an authored principle presumes another's substrate. The field makes substrate-ordering (the recurring 'author the registry before the budget gate' sequencing in decisions.md) machine-readable and lets adopters see, before adopting principle X, which principles must already be in place. All pre-existing principles were retrofitted with a dependencies array. See conventions.dependencies and the 2026-06-05 dependencies entry in decisions.md.

Schema document format_version 1.9 (2026-06-01) removes all catalogue content from taxonomy.json. taxonomy.json is now schema-only: it describes the per-principle field shapes, the enums, the spec blocks (principle_schema / ownership_spec / gates_spec / evidence_spec / change_history_spec / framework_mappings_spec), the conventions, and the usage notes. The catalogue's specific content — the five pillars + their focus areas (formerly the `pillars` array) and the AWS Lens alignment narrative (formerly `framework_alignment_notes`) — was removed. That content represents WHAT this catalogue authors against, not the schema itself, and belongs in a content-side artefact rather than the schema-side taxonomy file. If a future content file is created to hold pillars / focus areas / Lens alignment, taxonomy.json may add a `content_file_ref` pointer to it. Until then, `principle_schema.fields[].allowed_values_source` references that previously pointed at `pillars[]` paths are unresolvable — adopters reading the schema must know that pillar and focus-area enumerations live in a separate file (currently absent from this repository) or in the AWS Lens itself. `applies_to_principles_schema_version` unchanged at 1.9 — the per-principle field shapes are unchanged. See the 2026-06-01 content-removal entry in decisions.md.

Schema v1.11 (2026-06-06) added a new required root-level field `references` on every principle — an array of real-world evidence links, each `{ url, title, source, note, date }`, pointing at published incident write-ups, engineering postmortems, vendor case studies, or practitioner accounts that document the principle's failure mode actually occurring in industry. The field is the external, citable counterpart to `problem.examples` (which are synthetic, in-house illustrative scenarios): examples teach the failure shape, references prove it happens. The value is an empty array `[]` when no published write-up has yet been mapped to the principle's specific failure mode — empty is correct and expected, and a reference must genuinely document THIS principle's failure (not generic topic-adjacent content). Surfaced during the GC3B1-01 (prompt-token-budget) review when a web search turned up multiple real industry write-ups of unenforced-prompt-budget cost drift (Adaline, Opsmeter, ProjectDiscovery, Redis, Cycles); the catalogue had no structured slot to attach that evidence to the principle it validates. References render as hyperlinks in the runtime UI. All pre-existing principles were retrofitted: GC3B1-01 populated with the discovered links, the remaining twelve carry `[]` pending evidence. See conventions.references, references_spec, and the 2026-06-06 references entry in decisions.md.

principle_schema.intro: Every principle in principles.json conforms to this schema. This is what makes the catalogue different from cloud-vendor frameworks: each principle names who validates it, when it is checked, and what artefacts the validator inspects. Vendor frameworks stop at the question; this catalogue defines the audit.
principle_schema.vendor_neutrality_note: The schema is vendor-neutral. Pillar names match AWS / Azure / GCP WAF terminology because those names are de-facto industry-standard. The catalogue's primary anchor framework is the AWS Well-Architected Generative AI Lens, but the framework_mappings field is structured to hold parallel mappings to Azure, GCP, NIST, OWASP, or any other framework without schema change.

fields.name: principle_id
fields.description: Stable ID anchored to an AWS BP. Format: `<bp_code>-NN` where `bp_code` is a 5-character compression of the AWS pillar / question / BP triple (e.g. `GO1B1` = GenOps / Q1 / BP1; `GS4B2` = GenSec / Q4 / BP2) and `NN` is a two-digit sequential counter — the Nth principle promoted from that BP, in promotion order. IDs within a BP are consecutive with no gaps (e.g. GO1B1-01 / GO1B1-02 / GO1B1-03 / GO1B1-04). The AWS implementation step a principle concretises is recorded in `framework_mappings.aws.references[0].implementation_step.number`, not encoded in the principle_id suffix. Surfaces BP membership in the ID itself so siblings under one BP are recognisable at a glance. See conventions.principle_id_format. Legacy `PRIN_NNN` format is permitted on historical references (append-only journal entries) but new principles use the BP-anchored format.

fields.name: pillar
fields.allowed_values_source: pillars[].pillar_id formatted with the pillar name
fields.description: One of P1–P5. Maps to a GenAI Lens pillar. Pillar names are identical across AWS, Azure, and GCP WAFs.

fields.name: focus_area
fields.format: P## — Focus Area Name (e.g. 'P11 — Model Performance Evaluation')
fields.allowed_values_source: pillars[].focus_areas[].focus_area_id formatted with the focus area name
fields.description: Focus area code (e.g. P11 — Model Performance Evaluation). Internal taxonomy slot.

fields.name: impact_level
fields.description: Critical / High / Medium / Low. Drives criticality-tier applicability.

fields.name: applicability
fields.description: Object mapping AI system patterns to the principle's criticality for each pattern. Keys are drawn from `enums.applicability`: 'llm' (any LLM-based system — chatbot, summarisation, generation, prompt-based classification); 'rag' (retrieval-augmented generation — LLM + vector store + retrieval pipeline); 'agentic' (autonomous agents with tool use, planning, multi-step reasoning); 'ml' (traditional ML — supervised/unsupervised learning, classical models, embeddings as features). Values are drawn from `enums.pattern_criticality`: 'mandatory' (without this principle the failure mode it prevents is the dominant production-failure pattern for this kind of system) or 'nice_to_have' (the principle helps but the failure mode is rare for this pattern, or industry norms already enforce something equivalent). Omit patterns where the principle does not materially apply — absence means 'not applicable to this pattern'. `n/a` is not a value. A delivery team building a RAG system can filter the catalogue to `applicability.rag == 'mandatory'` to see their non-negotiable principles, or to any non-null `applicability.rag` for the full RAG-relevant set. Schema v1.7 (2026-05-26) reshaped this field from an array of patterns to a pattern-criticality map; the legacy v1.6 shape was `['agentic']`, the v1.7 shape is `{ 'agentic': 'mandatory' }`.

fields.name: serving_paradigm
fields.description: Object mapping foundation-model serving paradigms to the principle's criticality for each paradigm. Keys are drawn from `enums.serving_paradigm`: 'api_on_demand' (pure pay-per-token managed inference — OpenAI, Anthropic, Bedrock on-demand, Azure OpenAI on-demand); 'api_provisioned' (committed managed capacity — Bedrock Provisioned Throughput, Azure OpenAI PTUs, Vertex AI provisioned); 'self_hosted_managed' (your model on managed infrastructure — SageMaker AI endpoints, Vertex AI endpoints, Azure ML managed online endpoints); 'self_hosted_unmanaged' (your model on raw infrastructure — EC2 + vLLM, EKS / Slurm / HyperPod clusters, on-prem GPUs). Values are drawn from `enums.pattern_criticality`: 'mandatory' or 'nice_to_have'. Omit paradigms where the principle does not materially apply — absence means 'not applicable to this paradigm'. Orthogonal to `applicability` (which captures AI pattern: llm/rag/agentic/ml). A principle can apply to llm+rag+agentic AND only to self-hosted serving paradigms — the two filters compose. Schema v1.9 (2026-05-29) added this field after the GENCOST02-BP02 walkthrough surfaced that hosting-cost-optimisation principles need a serving-paradigm narrowing that applicability could not express. See conventions.choosing_serving_paradigm.

fields.name: maturity_level
fields.description: Organisational maturity at which this principle should be adopted. Values: 'foundational' (adopt on the first AI project; skipping it creates debt that compounds); 'scaling' (adopt once running multiple AI projects, roughly 2–5, or once multiple teams are involved); 'mature' (adopt once running an AI portfolio — 5+ projects, formal ARB, dedicated platform team). Pick the strictest level that honestly applies — if a principle is foundational for high-risk use cases but only scaling-relevant for low-risk ones, pick foundational. Safer to over-adopt than to under-adopt.

fields.name: dependencies
fields.item_structure: { principle_id: string, kind: string, reason: string }
fields.description: Array of other catalogue principles this principle DIRECTLY depends on — the substrate principles whose artefacts or enforcement machinery this principle's gates build on or reuse. Each entry has three fields: `principle_id` (the depended-on principle's id, e.g. 'GO3B1-01'); `kind` drawn from `enums.dependency_kind` ('hard' — this principle cannot be enforced or cannot function without the dependency's substrate in place; 'soft' — it composes with or is strengthened by the dependency but can be adopted independently); and `reason` (one sentence naming the concrete artefact or mechanism the dependency provides that this principle reuses or presumes). The value is an empty array `[]` for foundational principles that stand alone with no catalogue substrate. List only DIRECT dependencies — the transitive closure is recoverable by walking the graph, so do not duplicate a grandparent dependency that is already implied through a direct parent. Directional: A depends on B does not imply B depends on A. Added in schema v1.10 (2026-06-05) after the GENCOST03-BP03 walkthrough; see conventions.dependencies.

fields.name: references
fields.item_structure: { url: string, title: string, source: string, note: string, date: string }
fields.description: Real-world evidence that this principle's failure mode occurs in industry: published incident write-ups, engineering postmortems, vendor case studies, or practitioner accounts documenting the problem the principle prevents. Each entry: `url` (the link, must resolve to a public page); `title` (hyperlink display text — the page title or a short descriptive label); `source` (publisher or author, e.g. 'Adaline', 'ProjectDiscovery', 'Redis'); `note` (one sentence on what the link demonstrates and how it maps to this principle's failure mode); `date` (YYYY-MM-DD the link was captured/verified). Distinct from `problem.examples`, which are synthetic in-house illustrative scenarios — `references` are external, real, and citable. The value is an empty array `[]` when no published write-up has yet been mapped to this principle's specific failure mode; empty is correct and expected, and a reference must genuinely document THIS principle's failure rather than generic topic-adjacent content. Rendered as hyperlinks in the runtime UI. Added in schema v1.11 (2026-06-06); see conventions.references and references_spec.

fields.name: framework_mappings
fields.description: External-framework anchors keyed by framework (`aws`, `azure`, `gcp`, …); each framework holds a `references` array. See principle_schema.framework_mappings_spec for the full sub-schema. Replaces the legacy singular `aws_mapping` field that existed in schema versions <= 1.4.

fields.name: ownership
fields.description: Who validates the principle. See principle_schema.ownership_spec for tiers and qualification rules.

fields.name: statement
fields.description: The rule itself, written to be built against, not aspirational.

fields.name: problem
fields.description: The failure mode the principle prevents, with concrete examples.
notes: If the examples are illustrative narratives rather than documented incidents, signpost this in `description` (e.g. 'Examples below are illustrative — synthesised scenarios; specific numbers are not from cited evidence.') so a reader cannot mistake invented precision for audit-grade evidence. See conventions.illustrative_examples_signposting.

fields.name: solution
fields.description: How to implement, what good looks like.

fields.name: gates
fields.item_structure: { point: string, check: string, blocking: boolean }
fields.description: When the principle is checked in the lifecycle (pre-merge, pre-deploy, quarterly review) and what binary check fires at each point. See principle_schema.gates_spec for lifecycle points and the required-status-check constraint.
notes: For CI-enforced gates on project-tier principles, the `check` field must explicitly state that the check is configured as a required status check on the integration branch (not advisory) — without this, the branch-protection constraint is lost.

fields.name: evidence
fields.structure: { artefacts: array, review_mode: string, sign_off: string }
fields.description: What the central validator clicks on at a central review gate. Required for enterprise-tier principles. May be blank for project-tier principles, where the Gates block (CI enforcement, configured as a required status check) IS the proof. See principle_schema.evidence_spec for full structure.

fields.name: change_history
fields.description: Versioned audit trail of edits to this principle. See principle_schema.change_history_spec.

fields.name: explain_prompt
fields.structure: { system: string, user_template: string, compiled_for_principle_version: string, compiled_at: string (YYYY-MM-DD), compiled_by: string }
fields.description: Optional compiled OpenAI-style prompt pair used by the runtime UI's 'Explain' affordance to generate an illustrative example for a chosen industry and implementation type. `system` is the full system message; it bakes in the principle's identity, failure-mode shape (silent regression / setup absence / cumulative drift / one-shot breach), the output JSON contract, and the style rules. `user_template` is the user message with two Mustache-style placeholders that the runtime substitutes: `{{ industry }}` and `{{ implementation_type }}`. `compiled_for_principle_version` records which version of the principle the prompt was compiled against (mirrors change_history.current_version at compile time); a script can compare this against the current version to detect a stale prompt. `compiled_at` is the ISO date of compilation; `compiled_by` is a short identifier of the compiler (e.g. 'manual_authoring' or the meta-prompt version). Absent when the principle has not yet been compiled — the runtime can either fall back to a generic prompt or hide the Explain affordance. See conventions.per_principle_explain_prompt_compilation, usage.when_compiling_an_explain_prompt, and the schema-v1.8 entry in decisions.md.

framework_mappings_spec.intro: Each principle's relationship to external frameworks (AWS WAF Lenses, AIGP competency framework, Azure WAF, GCP WAF, NIST AI RMF, OWASP LLM Top 10, and similar) is captured in the framework_mappings field. The field is an object keyed by framework identifier; each framework key holds a references array of zero or more reference objects.
framework_mappings_spec.shape_example: framework_mappings: { aws: { references: [ ... ] }, aigp: { references: [ ... ] }, azure: { references: [ ... ] } }
framework_mappings_spec.primary_anchor_vs_cross_reference: AWS is the catalogue's primary anchor: every principle must concretise (or explicitly extend with mapping_state: na) an AWS GenAI Lens BP. AIGP and other frameworks (NIST, ISO 42001, EU AI Act, etc.) are cross-references — informational only, no enforcement obligation. This asymmetry is a convention, not encoded in the schema: every framework key under framework_mappings uses the same outer shape, and the inner structural fields are framework-specific. The convention is enforced by review, not by schema validation.

anchoring_methodology.scope: Applies to AWS framework references specifically (where implementation steps exist within BPs). Other frameworks like AIGP do not have an analogous step structure — AIGP references map at the competency level directly.
anchoring_methodology.explanation: Principles in this catalogue anchor at the AWS implementation-step level by default, not the BP level. Each AWS BP is decomposed into its constituent implementation steps, and each step receives a due-diligence pass to decide whether it warrants a standalone principle. Not all steps become principles in this catalogue — some are too tactical to elevate, some are vendor-specific menu options rather than mandates, some are covered as implementation detail inside a sibling principle's solution.approach or gates. The per-BP due-diligence record (which steps were promoted to principles, which were not, and the rationale for each) lives in lens_mapping.md.
anchoring_methodology.rationale: Anchoring at the BP level produces statements that sound architectural but are too broad to verify — the value-add over AWS becomes hard to see. Anchoring at the implementation-step level makes the lineage obvious (each principle is recognisably a single AWS instruction made enforceable) and forces every principle to earn its place by being architecturally distinct. The trade-off is catalogue size: a fully-elaborated catalogue at step-level granularity is large. This is accepted because adopters do not consume the whole catalogue — they pick the BPs and steps relevant to their workload and maturity.
anchoring_methodology.positioning: This catalogue is a worked example, not a comprehensive standard. The schema is the product; the specific decomposition decisions in our catalogue are illustrative. An organisation adopting it may inherit our decomposition decisions, override them, or extend with steps we deliberately did not promote. A 'missing' implementation step in our catalogue means we judged it not concrete enough to elevate at our scope — not that nobody should ever elevate it. Adopters are expected to make their own calls.
anchoring_methodology.exception_whole_bp_anchoring: A principle may exceptionally anchor to an entire BP (no implementation_step) when the BP is simple enough that step-level decomposition adds no clarity. This should be rare; the reason must be documented in the reference's note field and reflected in the BP's lens_mapping.md entry.

cardinality_notes: Default cardinality: one reference, one implementation_step. Most principles concretise exactly one AWS implementation step within one BP.
A principle can map to multiple frameworks (an entry for `aws` and one for `azure` etc.).

A principle can map to multiple steps within a single BP using multiple references in the same framework's references array. This should be the exception, not the default — if you find yourself reaching for it often, the principle may be doing too much and is a candidate for decomposition.
A principle can have a single reference with implementation_step: null when it exceptionally covers an entire BP. Rare; document why in the note.
A principle can have a single reference with mapping_state: 'na' when no analogue exists in this framework. Structural fields and implementation_step are null.
Only framework keys that have been audited for this principle should be present. If Azure hasn't been checked, the azure key is absent rather than present with an empty references array.
framework_mappings_spec.related_file: lens_mapping.md is the per-BP due-diligence ledger: for every AWS BP, it lists each implementation step with a status (promoted_to_principle, not_promoted with rationale, or pending_review).
framework_mappings_spec.reference_object_structure: Every reference object — regardless of framework — has three common fields (mapping_state, last_checked, note) plus a set of framework-specific structural fields. The common fields are required everywhere. The framework-specific fields vary because each framework has its own internal taxonomy (AWS has lens / pillar / question / BP / implementation_step; AIGP has domain / competency; NIST would have something else).

common_reference_fields.name: mapping_state
common_reference_fields.description: One of verified, unverified, na. See mapping_states below.

common_reference_fields.name: last_checked
common_reference_fields.description: ISO date when this reference was last assessed against the source framework. Populated on creation; updated on each verification pass. Required regardless of mapping_state.

common_reference_fields.name: note
common_reference_fields.description: Free-text rationale: what our principle adds beyond the framework's guidance, why the mapping is provisional, why no analogue exists, or any cross-lens placement explanation.

aws.name: lens
aws.description: The AWS Lens name (e.g. 'Generative AI Lens', 'Responsible AI Lens'). Null for references with mapping_state: na.

aws.name: pillar
aws.description: The AWS pillar code and name (e.g. 'GENOPS — Operational Excellence').

aws.name: focus_area
aws.description: The AWS question-area / focus-area title (e.g. 'Model performance evaluation').

aws.name: question
aws.description: The full AWS question code and text (e.g. 'GENOPS01 — How do you achieve and verify consistent model output quality?').

aws.name: best_practice
aws.description: The AWS BP code and title (e.g. 'GENOPS01-BP01 — Periodically evaluate functional performance').

aws.name: implementation_step
aws.structure: { number: integer, title: string, verbatim_text: string | null }
aws.description: The specific AWS implementation step within the BP that this reference scopes to. Implementation-step anchoring is the default for AWS — principles concretise specific steps, not whole BPs. See framework_mappings_spec.anchoring_methodology. `number` is the step number as numbered in the AWS BP page. `title` is the step's title. `verbatim_text` is optional and holds the AWS-original step text when useful for traceability. Set to null only when a principle exceptionally covers an entire BP without step-level decomposition (rare; document the reason in `note`).

aigp.name: domain
aigp.description: The AIGP domain code and title (e.g. 'Domain III: Understanding How to Govern AI Development'). Null for references with mapping_state: na.

aigp.name: competency
aigp.description: The AIGP competency code and title within the domain (e.g. 'III.A: Govern Data for AI Systems'). Null for references with mapping_state: na.

mapping_states.verified: The framework BP has been confirmed against the source documentation. All structural fields are populated with real values. Our principle is the concrete enforceable version of the BP.
mapping_states.unverified: Provisional mapping based on subject-matter proximity. Structural fields may contain TODO placeholders. Needs a verification pass against the framework's source documentation.
mapping_states.na: No analogue exists in this framework. The framework was checked; nothing matched. Structural fields are null; note carries the closest-fit explanation if useful. Used for native extensions and for principles AWS would cover only in a different Lens that's also not analogous.

framework_mappings_spec.cross_lens_placement: Some principles cover topics AWS places in the Responsible AI Lens rather than the GenAI Lens. These appear as an entry in framework_mappings.aws.references with lens: 'Responsible AI Lens'. The note field explains the cross-lens placement. There is no separate top-level cross-reference field — the cross-lens fact is implicit in the reference's lens value. (The legacy responsible_ai_lens_crossref top-level field that existed in schema versions <= 1.4 has been retired; see decisions.md 2026-05-26 entry.)

ownership_spec.intro: Two tiers, set on each principle via `ownership.tier`. Tier reflects CENTRALISATION OF ENFORCEMENT INFRASTRUCTURE — whether the platform team builds and maintains the runners, lints, validators, CI workflow templates, dashboards, etc. that make the principle hold mechanically (enterprise) or each project team builds its own (project). Tier is an INDEPENDENT axis from `validator` and `audit_mode`: an enterprise-tier principle can still have `validator: project_architect` and `audit_mode: self_attestation_with_mechanical_evidence` when the platform team owns the tooling and the PA self-attests using it. Tier is determined via the V1 tier rubric at `agentflow/sections/tier/rubric.json`. Convention introduced 2026-05-31; see `conventions.tier_rubric` and the 2026-05-31 entry in decisions.md.

tiers.project: Each project team builds its own enforcement tooling (validators, runners, lints, CI workflows) for this principle. PA self-attests using locally-built tooling. Appropriate when the tooling is small (a single small validator, a fill-in template) such that duplicate effort across projects is cheap and standardisation cost would exceed reuse savings. Examples in the current catalogue: GO1B1-05 (refresh log schema + small validator); GC1B1-01 (ADR template + fill-in lint).
tiers.enterprise: The platform team owns the enforcement infrastructure (runners, lints, validators, CI workflow templates, dashboards). Project teams consume the platform team's tooling rather than rebuilding. Validation may still be PA self-attestation against the centralised tooling (the typical case under the new tier semantics), OR may escalate to ARB / Security / Legal / Finance when legal exposure is in scope (D1 of the tier rubric fires). Examples in the current catalogue: GO1B1-01 (eval runner / diff tool / CI template / manifest validator); GO1B1-02 (strata validator / per-stratum threshold checker / coverage lint); GO1B1-03 (metric-encapsulation lint / runner-import contract); GO1B1-04 (drift infra / baseline schema / alert routing); GC2B2-01 (IaC-vs-config lint with multi-cloud parser / deployed-alarm verification).

ownership_spec.tier_rubric_ref: `agentflow/sections/tier/rubric.json`. Two dimensions: D1 (legal_exposure, binary veto — regulator-in-scope / external-audit / cross-jurisdictional-data-residency forces centralisation regardless of D2) and D2 (repeatability_cost, 0-3 — how much engineering each project would repeat to satisfy the principle locally). Three outcomes mapped to the two-value tier enum: `mandatory_centralise` (D1=yes) → tier: enterprise; `recommended_centralise` (D1=no, D2≥2) → tier: enterprise; `local` (D1=no, D2≤1) → tier: project. Tie-breaker at D2=1 defaults to local — cheaper to centralise later than decentralise a poorly-scoped platform. The three-outcome rubric versus two-value enum keeps the rubric expressive while the catalogue schema stays simple; if mandatory-vs-recommended granularity needs to surface at the schema level later, a sub-field can be added without breaking the tier enum.
ownership_spec.supersedes_prior_qualification_rules: The prior 4-rule binary screen (failure spans multiple projects / regulator in scope / specialist function owns control / sign-off authority gap) is superseded by the tier rubric. The 4 rules are subsumed: rule 1 ≈ D2 high; rule 2 = D1 veto; rule 3 folds into D2; rule 4 folds into D1. The new rubric makes reuse cost a first-class scored dimension rather than a binary trigger. Convention introduced 2026-05-31; see the corresponding entry in decisions.md for the reasoning and the per-principle reclassification table (5 principles reclassified project → enterprise: GO1B1-01, GO1B1-02, GO1B1-03, GO1B1-04, GC2B2-01).
ownership_spec.rule: A principle's tier is the outcome of the tier rubric mapped to the two-value enum. See `tier_rubric_ref` above. Validator and audit_mode are independent axes — set them based on the validation mechanism actually used for the principle. The common case under the new tier semantics is enterprise-tier + project_architect validator + self_attestation_with_mechanical_evidence audit_mode: platform team owns the tooling, PA self-attests using it.

ownership_object_fields.name: tier
ownership_object_fields.description: project or enterprise. Decided via the tier rubric at `agentflow/sections/tier/rubric.json`. Reflects centralisation of enforcement infrastructure, not validation.

ownership_object_fields.name: validator
ownership_object_fields.description: Named validator role (e.g. project_architect, enterprise_security, finance_and_arb, legal_and_responsible_ai). Independent of tier — an enterprise-tier principle can have validator: project_architect when the platform team builds the tooling and the PA self-attests using it.

ownership_object_fields.name: audit_mode
ownership_object_fields.description: How the validator audits (e.g. self_attestation_with_mechanical_evidence, central_review_at_gate). Drives whether `evidence` artefacts are required: `central_review_at_gate` audit_mode means a central validator clicks on artefacts and `evidence` is required; `self_attestation_with_mechanical_evidence` means CI is the proof and `evidence` may be empty.

ownership_object_fields.name: arb_role
ownership_object_fields.description: What ARB's role is for this principle (e.g. dashboard_and_spot_check, primary_reviewer).

gates_spec.intro: Each gate is a { point, check, blocking } triple. point is the lifecycle moment the check fires. check is a binary statement that either holds or doesn't. blocking indicates whether failure prevents progression.
gates_spec.lifecycle_points_note: Lifecycle point depends on what the principle is checking, not directly on tier. Per-PR or per-deploy disciplines (eval harness, drift baseline, ADR existence) typically gate at pre_merge or pre_deploy regardless of tier; cadence-driven or central-review disciplines (portfolio reviews, audit-gated sign-offs) gate at release, quarterly_review, or annual_review. Under the new tier semantics (2026-05-31), enterprise-tier principles often still gate at pre_merge — the enterprise tier reflects that the platform team owns the tooling, not that ARB clicks evidence URLs at a central gate.
gates_spec.required_status_check_constraint: For project-tier principles where the gate is enforced via CI, the check field must explicitly state that the check is configured as a REQUIRED status check on the integration branch (typically `develop` in git-flow setups, `main` in trunk-based setups) — not advisory. Without this in-gate constraint, a PA can write a passing CI check that nobody is forced to wait for, and the gate quietly becomes optional. This is what carries the branch-protection requirement now that Evidence is optional for project-tier principles.

evidence_spec.intro: Evidence enumerates the artefacts a central validator (ARB, Security, Legal, Finance) physically clicks on at a central review gate. Under the new tier semantics (2026-05-31), evidence is required when the `ownership.audit_mode` is `central_review_at_gate` (a central validator actually clicks artefacts), NOT automatically when the tier is enterprise. An enterprise-tier principle with `audit_mode: self_attestation_with_mechanical_evidence` may leave evidence blank — CI is the proof. The trigger for non-empty evidence is the audit mechanism, not the tier.

enterprise_tier_shape.location_requirement: A literal path, URL pattern, or git query — something the validator can click or run.
enterprise_tier_shape.check_meaning: What makes the artefact pass: existence, freshness, content shape, growth trend, author distribution, ADR coverage, etc.

project_tier_shape.rule: The Gates block (CI enforcement, configured as a required status check on the integration branch) IS the proof. Evidence may be empty (artefacts: [], review_mode: 'automated_only'). There is no external validator routinely clicking URLs; the build system is the validator. The branch-protection meta-config lives inside the gate's check field, not as a separate Evidence artefact.

review_modes.central_review_at_gate: ARB / Specialist convenes a review session.
review_modes.automated_only: CI alone is sufficient — project-tier default when Evidence is blank.

evidence_spec.sign_off_rule: sign_off is binary — pass or fail, no graded scale. If a principle needs a graded scale, it's two principles.

change_history_spec.intro: Every principle carries a change_history field tracking its evolution over time.
change_history_spec.shape_example: change_history: { current_version: '1.0.0', changes: [ { version, date, author, summary } ] }
change_history_spec.current_version: Semantic version of the principle as it stands today. Mirrors the version field of the most recent entry in changes. Denormalised on purpose: a reader can see the current version without scanning the array.
change_history_spec.changes: Append-only array, chronological (oldest first, newest last).

change_entry_fields.name: version
change_entry_fields.description: MAJOR for rule changes (statement rewritten, scope changed). MINOR for meaningful additions (new gate, new evidence requirement, new framework mapping, mapping promoted from unverified to verified). PATCH for clarifications, typo fixes, note additions.

change_entry_fields.name: date
change_entry_fields.description: ISO date the change was made.

change_entry_fields.name: author
change_entry_fields.description: Name of the person who made the change.

change_entry_fields.name: summary
change_entry_fields.description: Single sentence describing what changed. Should be specific enough that a reader scanning the history can tell what each version did.

change_history_spec.append_only_rule: Never edit prior entries. Append a new entry for every change.

references_spec.intro: The references field holds external, real-world evidence that the principle's failure mode is not hypothetical — published incidents, engineering postmortems, vendor case studies, practitioner write-ups. It is the citable counterpart to problem.examples (which are synthetic, in-house illustrations). examples teach the failure shape; references prove it happens.

entry_fields.name: url
entry_fields.description: The link. Must resolve to a public page.

entry_fields.name: title
entry_fields.description: Hyperlink display text — the article/page title or a short descriptive label.

entry_fields.name: note
entry_fields.description: One sentence: what the link demonstrates and how it maps to this principle's failure mode.

references_spec.authoring_rule: Only add a reference when the linked material genuinely documents THIS principle's failure mode — not generic topic-adjacent content. A reference that does not map to the specific failure the principle prevents is noise. Empty array [] is correct and expected until matching evidence is found.
references_spec.distinct_from_problem_examples: problem.examples are synthetic scenarios authored to illustrate the failure shape; references are external, real, and citable. The two are complementary: examples teach the shape, references prove it happens.

when_starting_work_on_a_new_aws_bp: Read the BP page from the live AWS Lens; capture the implementation steps verbatim.
Add or update the BP's entry in lens_mapping.md, listing every implementation step.
For each step, decide: promote to its own principle, merge into a sibling principle, or omit. Record the decision and rationale per step in lens_mapping.md.
Author one principle per promoted step. Each principle's framework_mappings.aws.references[0].implementation_step points to the specific step the principle concretises.
Steps that are not promoted leave a documented gap in lens_mapping.md so adopters can see what we considered and chose to leave to them.
when_authoring_a_principle: Set `pillar` to one of P1–P5 from pillars[].pillar_id, formatted as 'P# — Pillar Name'.
Set `focus_area` to one of the focus-area codes under the chosen pillar, formatted as 'P## — Focus Area Name'.

Focus areas are mutually exclusive within a pillar. A principle gets exactly one focus area, even if it touches a second. If a principle truly spans two focus areas equally, that is usually a signal that the principle is too broad and should be split.

Statement should be a recognisable derivation of the anchored AWS implementation step's wording, made enforceable. A reader comparing the AWS step text to our statement should see how ours concretises theirs — not have to take it on faith.
If a future principle clearly doesn't fit any existing focus area, add a new focus area to this taxonomy file rather than forcing the fit.
usage.when_drafting_problem_examples: Examples should be concrete failure patterns. If the examples are illustrative narratives invented for clarity rather than drawn from documented incidents, signpost this in problem.description so readers don't mistake invented precision for cited evidence.
usage.when_promoting_a_mapping_from_unverified: Update the reference's mapping_state to 'verified', refresh last_checked to today's date, append a minor-version entry to change_history with a summary describing what was verified and against which source URL.
usage.when_a_new_framework_mapping_is_added: Add a new top-level key to framework_mappings (e.g. azure) with at least one reference. Bump change_history with a minor version entry.
when_compiling_an_explain_prompt: Identify the principle's failure shape before drafting. Common shapes are silent_regression (a working chain breaks while surface metrics stay plausible — PRIN_001, PRIN_002, PRIN_003 all sit here), setup_absence (the discipline was never built; exposure event makes it visible — a centralised-SDK or fallback-model principle would sit here), cumulative_drift (months of locally-rational decisions add up to a portfolio-level problem — vendor sprawl, cache hit-rate budget), and one_shot_breach (a single bad PR or unauthenticated call creates exposure — agent-to-agent auth, prompt-injection defence). The compiled prompt's failure-mode framing must match the shape; using a silent-regression scaffold for a setup-absence principle forces the LLM to invent a fictional working chain and the example contradicts itself.

Bake the principle's identity into the system prompt — title, statement, the specific failure mode it prevents, the catalogue's existing examples as references (with the instruction not to repeat them verbatim), and the applicability map. The user_template should hold only the runtime variables `{{ industry }}` and `{{ implementation_type }}`; the principle data does not need to be re-passed at call time.

Carry the invariant style rules in every compiled prompt: JSON-only output of the agreed shape (use_case_background + issue_detail); no bullet lists, headings, or markdown beyond the optional italicised opening sentence; no invented regulatory specifics; honest signposting of invented numbers; the implementation-type definitions (RAG retrieves; Agent reasons over steps and calls tools; LLM Only is single-turn or chained without retrieval or tools; ML is classical with no LLM in the loop); the rule that if the chosen implementation type is not one the principle's applicability map marks mandatory, the example opens with an italicised illustrative-only caveat.

Carry the discriminating-failure-mode discipline: the example must demonstrate a failure that THIS principle uniquely catches and that simpler controls (aggregate accuracy, basic monitoring, output-equality checks) would miss. If the prompted failure would be caught by trivial monitoring, the example accidentally argues the principle is unnecessary. For silent_regression principles add the working-baseline + broken-link + surface-stays-plausible scaffold; for setup_absence add the team-belief-vs-exposure-event scaffold; for cumulative_drift add the many-locally-rational-decisions scaffold; for one_shot_breach add the never-tested-until-exploited scaffold.

Populate the metadata fields: `compiled_for_principle_version` mirrors the principle's `change_history.current_version` at compile time; `compiled_at` is today's date; `compiled_by` is a short identifier ('manual_authoring' for hand-compiled prompts in the catalogue's early life, or the meta-prompt version once a formal meta-prompt exists).

Re-compile when the principle's statement, problem, examples, applicability, or maturity_level change in a way that affects what the example should show. A pure clarification or typo PATCH does not require re-compile; a MINOR or MAJOR bump usually does. Compare `compiled_for_principle_version` against `change_history.current_version` to detect stale prompts.

conventions.illustrative_examples_signposting: If problem.examples are illustrative narratives rather than documented incidents, signpost this in problem.description (e.g. 'Examples below are illustrative — synthesised scenarios; specific numbers are not from cited evidence.') so a reader cannot mistake invented precision for audit-grade evidence. Convention added 2026-05-26 after PRIN_001's first revision; see decisions.md 2026-05-26 entry.
conventions.statement_must_derive_from_anchored_step: A principle's statement.title and statement.description must be a recognisable derivation of its primary framework_mappings reference's implementation_step (or, in the rare whole-BP-anchored case, the BP title). A reader comparing the AWS step text and our statement should be able to see how ours concretises theirs — not have to take it on faith. If the lineage is not visible, the principle is either mis-anchored or the statement is a different principle wearing the wrong reference. Convention added 2026-05-26 after PRIN_001's first statement composed from BP implementation guidance rather than from any one step.
conventions.step_level_anchoring_default: Principles anchor at the AWS implementation-step level by default. See framework_mappings_spec.anchoring_methodology for full rationale. Per-BP due-diligence (which steps were promoted, which omitted, why) is recorded in lens_mapping.md.
conventions.catalogue_is_worked_example: This catalogue is a worked example of the schema, not a comprehensive standard. Adopters are expected to inherit, override, or extend our decomposition decisions for their own context. A step we did not promote to a principle in our catalogue is a documented gap, not a prohibition.
conventions.exemplar_principle: PRIN_001 in principles.json (currently authored at BP-level granularity) will be re-anchored or decomposed under the step-level methodology. Until that re-anchoring lands, the v1.5 schema's worked exemplar is PRIN_001's pre-decomposition form; the post-decomposition exemplars will be its successor principles.
conventions.applicability_and_maturity_level: Every principle declares which AI patterns it applies to (`applicability` — a pattern-criticality map) and at what organisational maturity it should be adopted (`maturity_level`). These are filtering aids for adopters, not gating fields: a startup running a single LLM chatbot can filter the catalogue to `applicability.llm == 'mandatory'` and `maturity_level: 'foundational'` and pick up exactly the principles that are non-negotiable on day one, without being overwhelmed by mature-portfolio concerns. Convention re-introduced in schema v1.6 (2026-05-26); the fields existed in the legacy v1.0 schema, were dropped during the v1.5 rebuild while framework_mappings was being finalised, and brought back when the GENOPS01-BP01 walkthrough surfaced the need. Schema v1.7 (same day) reshaped `applicability` from a flat array `['agentic']` to a pattern-criticality map `{ 'agentic': 'mandatory' }` so adopters can distinguish mandatory-for-pattern from nice-to-have-for-pattern, and the two fields stay orthogonal: `applicability` map values say 'for THIS system pattern, how critical is this principle'; `maturity_level` says 'at what point in your org's GenAI journey should you adopt it.' A principle can be `mandatory` for a pattern and still be `scaling` rather than `foundational` overall.
conventions.choosing_pattern_criticality: When tagging a principle's `applicability` map, choose criticality per pattern by asking: 'is the failure mode this principle prevents the *dominant production-failure pattern* for this kind of system?' If yes, `mandatory`. If the failure mode is real but rare for this pattern, or industry norms already enforce something equivalent (e.g. stratified k-fold for traditional ML, train/test splits for any supervised model), `nice_to_have`. Omit patterns where the principle does not materially apply — absence means 'not applicable', and `n/a` is not a value. A principle can be `mandatory` for one pattern and `nice_to_have` for another; that is expected and exactly what the map shape exists to express. Convention added in schema v1.7 (2026-05-26).
conventions.per_principle_explain_prompt_compilation: Every principle carries its own compiled `explain_prompt` (system + user_template) rather than relying on a single universal runtime prompt. A universal prompt cannot honour every failure shape in the catalogue: scaffolding that works for silent-regression principles (working baseline → broken link → plausible surface) actively distorts setup-absence principles (team belief → exposure event), cumulative-drift principles (many locally-rational decisions → portfolio-level problem), and one-shot-breach principles (never tested until exploited). Compiling per principle moves the failure-shape calibration off the runtime path and into authoring time, where it can be done deliberately, reviewed, and stored. The compiled prompt is principle-specific in content (identity, statement, problem, examples baked in) but uniform in shape (system + user_template, JSON output contract, same style rules) so the runtime UI handles every principle identically. The runtime user message carries only `{{ industry }}` and `{{ implementation_type }}` — the rest lives in the system prompt. Convention added in schema v1.8 (2026-05-26). See usage.when_compiling_an_explain_prompt for the compilation method and the schema-v1.8 entry in decisions.md for the architectural rationale.
conventions.per_field_authoring_rubrics: Every LLM-authored field in a principle — statement, problem, solution, gates, explain_prompt, focus_area, impact_level, applicability, maturity_level, framework_mappings (ten fields) — carries a 0–3 dimensioned quality rubric that the authoring pipeline runs against the field's draft before any downstream field is authored. Each rubric defines named dimensions, a 0–3 scoring scale per dimension, a deterministic threshold rule (V1: all dimensions ≥ 2 to pass), a judge prompt template, and calibration examples drawn from the catalogue's own history. Deterministic / rules-based fields — principle_id, pillar, evidence, change_history, ownership (five fields) — do not have rubrics; there is no judgment to evaluate. Each LLM-authored field is implemented as three JSON files under `agentflow/sections/<section>/` — `generate.json` (authoring prompt), `rubric.json` (judge prompt + dimensions + threshold + calibration), `revise.json` (failed-rubric revision prompt). Shared principle-level content lives at `agentflow/system_prompts/{generate,rubric,revise}.json` and is composed at runtime with each section file. The rubric framework is catalogue-authoring discipline — what the agentflow pipeline does when producing catalogue content — not a workload-architecture rule, so it does not appear as a principle in principles.json. Convention added 2026-05-27 after the titles failure: PRIN_001, PRIN_002, PRIN_003, and a draft PRIN_004 title all shipped noun-phrase (non-prescriptive) statement.title values because there was no structured judge to catch the form flaw at draft time. The first rubric authored under this convention is `agentflow/sections/statement/rubric.json` (five dimensions: is_prescriptive, derives_from_aws_verbatim, names_artefact_and_enforcement, scope_match, parallel_form_with_siblings); originally authored at `agentflow/rubrics/statement.md` on 2026-05-27, migrated to the sections/ structure the same day. Subsequent field rubrics are deferred — built as their respective agentflow authoring nodes are wired, not all upfront, to avoid the universal-prompt mistake the catalogue learned from explain_prompt. See the 2026-05-27 rubric structure migration entry in decisions.md.
conventions.principle_id_format: A principle's `principle_id` is a BP-anchored, sequential code: `<bp_code>-NN`. The `bp_code` is a 5-character compression of the AWS pillar / question / BP triple — two letters for the pillar (GO = GenOps, GS = GenSec, GR = GenRel, GP = GenPerf, GC = GenCost), one digit for the question number within the pillar, the letter B, one digit for the BP number within the question. Examples: `GO1B1` = GenOps Q1 BP1 (GENOPS01-BP01); `GS4B2` = GenSec Q4 BP2 (GENSEC04-BP02); `GC5B1` = GenCost Q5 BP1 (GENCOST05-BP01). The `NN` suffix is a two-digit sequential counter — the Nth principle promoted from that BP, in promotion order. IDs within a BP are consecutive (01, 02, 03, …) with no gaps. Examples from the current catalogue: `GO1B1-01` was the first principle promoted from GENOPS01-BP01 (anchors AWS step 1); `GO1B1-02` was the second (anchors step 2); `GO1B1-03` was the third (anchors step 4 — step 3 was not_promoted but the suffix does not skip); `GO1B1-04` was the fourth (anchors step 6 — steps 3 and 5 were not_promoted but the suffix does not skip). The AWS implementation step a principle concretises is recorded in `framework_mappings.aws.references[0].implementation_step.number`, not encoded in the principle_id suffix. This convention surfaces BP membership in the ID itself: siblings under one BP share the `<bp_code>` prefix and are recognisable at a glance, eliminating the need to read `framework_mappings.aws.references[0].best_practice` on each principle to recover the BP-shaped view. Why sequential rather than step-mirroring: consecutive IDs make the catalogue feel ordered (no gaps from not_promoted steps); the AWS step anchor information is already preserved in `framework_mappings`, so encoding it in the suffix would duplicate data. Edge case: if AWS adds a Q10+ in any pillar, the question digit becomes 2 chars and the bp_code grows to 6 characters; the pattern survives but loses its 5-char tidiness. Convention added 2026-05-27, replacing the legacy `PRIN_NNN` format. The four existing principles (formerly PRIN_001 / PRIN_002 / PRIN_003 / PRIN_004) were renamed to GO1B1-01 / GO1B1-02 / GO1B1-03 / GO1B1-04 in the same session with PATCH bumps. Historical references in append-only journals were left under the legacy IDs; live cross-references were updated. See the 2026-05-27 rename entry in decisions.md.
conventions.choosing_serving_paradigm: When tagging a principle's `serving_paradigm` map, ask per paradigm: 'does the failure mode this principle prevents exist for workloads on this serving paradigm?' If yes and the failure is the dominant production-failure pattern for this paradigm, `mandatory`. If the failure exists but is mild for this paradigm, or industry norms already enforce something equivalent, `nice_to_have`. Omit paradigms where the failure mode does not exist — absence means 'not applicable to this paradigm', and `n/a` is not a value. A principle can be `mandatory` for self_hosted_managed + self_hosted_unmanaged and omit api_on_demand entirely (e.g. GC2B2-01 — right-size hosting infrastructure: there is no hosting infrastructure to right-size on pure-API workloads); a principle can be `mandatory` across all four paradigms (e.g. every eval-discipline principle in GO1B1: the harness sits in the workload repository regardless of how the model is served). The field is orthogonal to `applicability` (AI pattern) and `maturity_level` (org-level adoption point) — together the three filters let an adopter narrow the catalogue to 'mandatory principles for my pattern + my serving paradigm + my maturity'. Convention added in schema v1.9 (2026-05-29). See the 2026-05-29 serving_paradigm entry in decisions.md for the rationale (GENCOST02-BP02 walkthrough where GC2B2-01's self-hosting-only scope could not be expressed by applicability alone).
conventions.dependencies: Every principle declares a `dependencies` array recording the other catalogue principles it DIRECTLY depends on. A dependency is the relationship 'principle A's enforcement presumes principle B's substrate is already in place' — typically A's gate reuses an artefact, field, or piece of tooling that B mandates into existence. Two kinds: `hard` (A cannot be enforced or cannot function without B — e.g. GC3B1-01's token-budget gate has no addressable `template_id` to bite on unless GO3B1-01's template registry exists first; GO1B1-06's re-eval-on-model-change gate cannot run without GO1B1-01's eval harness) and `soft` (A composes with or is strengthened by B but can be adopted on its own). Record only DIRECT edges: if A hard-depends on B and B hard-depends on C, A lists B but not C — the chain to C is recovered by walking B's own dependencies. The relationship is directional and asymmetric (A→B does not imply B→A) and must be acyclic (a dependency cycle means two principles are mutually unenforceable and is a decomposition smell to resolve, not record). Foundational substrate principles that stand alone carry `[]`. The field makes the substrate-ordering discipline that recurs throughout decisions.md (the 'author the registry before the budget gate', 'pin models before re-eval' sequencing calls) machine-readable: an adopter can compute, for any principle they want, the full set of principles that must already hold; and an author adding a new principle can see at a glance which substrate it leans on. Each `reason` should name the concrete inherited mechanism, not restate the relationship abstractly ('reuses GO3B1-01's per-template manifest row + tokenizer adapters', not 'depends on GO3B1-01'). Convention added in schema v1.10 (2026-06-05) during the GENCOST03-BP03 walkthrough. See the 2026-06-05 dependencies entry in decisions.md.
conventions.step_promotion_rubric: Separate from `per_field_authoring_rubrics` (which scores quality WITHIN an already-promoted principle's authored fields), the step_promotion rubric scores the upstream binary decision of whether an AWS implementation step becomes a principle at all. It is the first node in the agentflow pipeline for any BP walkthrough — runs on each AWS implementation step in turn and ratifies a draft promote / not_promote decision before any per-field authoring node fires. Implemented as `agentflow/sections/step_promotion/{generate,rubric,revise}.json` (co-located under sections/ for file-layout uniformity, though conceptually it is NOT a section of a principle — it is an upstream pipeline decision before any principle exists; if the agentflow directory is ever restructured, an `agentflow/pipeline_nodes/` peer would be the natural home). Four dimensions: `has_enforceable_artefact` (the step concretises to a named artefact in the workload repository plus a CI gate or evidence check that can fire on it; pure decision-process advice like 'identify your requirements' fails this dimension), `architecturally_distinct` (substance is unique within the BP and not redundant with a sibling principle elsewhere in the catalogue; steps that restate or directly apply a prior step fail), `in_bp_scope` (substance belongs to this BP / pillar rather than a different pillar's BP; cross-pillar substance is not_promoted here and earmarked for the target BP), `not_vendor_menu` (generic architectural mandate, not a list of vendor service options like 'consider X, explore Y'). Threshold: all dimensions ≥ 2 to ratify. Binary outcome — promote or not_promote, no `merge` category. If a not_promoted step's content is still architecturally relevant, the sibling principle that absorbs it captures the substance in its own statement / solution / gates content; that is a sibling-authoring choice, not a third structural outcome of this decomposition pass. Calibration corpus drawn from existing precedent: BP01 steps 1 / 2 / 4 / 6 / 7 (promoted) and steps 3 / 5 (not_promoted); GENOPS02-BP01 step 7 (not_promote — cross-pillar, scoring 2/2/3/1 → fail and routed to revise as a calibration of how the cross-pillar reasoning should engage with the vendor-menu language); GENCOST01-BP01 steps 1 / 2 (not_promote — decision-process advice and absorbed shortlist); plus a hypothetical 'requirements YAML' promote for that same step 1 that scores 1/2/2/3 → fail as a counter-example. Convention added 2026-05-29 after the GENCOST01-BP01 walkthrough surfaced that hand-applied promote/not_promote calls had no structured judge — the same gap shape that per_field_authoring_rubrics filled for the within-principle authoring side. See the 2026-05-29 step_promotion entry in decisions.md and the CLAUDE.md hand-apply rule until the agentflow LangGraph pipeline is wired.
conventions.references: Every principle declares a `references` array holding external, real-world evidence that its failure mode actually occurs — published incident write-ups, engineering postmortems, vendor case studies, practitioner accounts. It is the citable, external counterpart to `problem.examples`, which are synthetic scenarios authored in-house to illustrate the failure shape: examples teach the shape, references prove it happens. Each entry names `url`, `title` (the hyperlink text), `source` (publisher/author), `note` (one sentence mapping the link to this principle's specific failure mode), and `date` (capture date, YYYY-MM-DD). The discipline that keeps the field honest: only attach a reference that genuinely documents THIS principle's failure mode, not generic topic-adjacent material — a loosely-related link is noise that dilutes the catalogue's evidentiary value. An empty array `[]` is the correct and expected state until matching evidence is found; do not pad the field. References surface as hyperlinks in the runtime UI, giving an ARB reviewer or adopter a one-click path from the principle to a real industry account of the cost of ignoring it. Convention added in schema v1.11 (2026-06-06) during the GC3B1-01 review, when a web search surfaced multiple real write-ups of unenforced-prompt-budget cost drift and the catalogue had no structured slot to attach them. See references_spec and the 2026-06-06 references entry in decisions.md.
